---
title: "Filtering Tables"
engine: knitr
filters:
  - interactive-duckdb
databases:
  - name: subscriptions
    path: "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Alaburda/data-analyst-sql-cookbook/master/databases/carwash.duckdb"
    format: duckdb
---

```{r setup}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(duckdb)

con <- dbConnect(duckdb(), "../databases/subscriptions.duckdb", read_only = TRUE)

```

Everyone starts out with filtering using the WHERE clause but there's more to it than just specifying columns and their conditions.   

# Anti joins

Anti joins are a type of join where you return only rows that do not match any row in a given table. For example, let's say you have users who have done one time purchases but who have no subscription service. You want to send a marketing email to users who don't have a subscription service yet but have done a one time purchase.

```{.sql .interactive .subscriptions}


```

# Filtering on the ON clause

You can use the ON clause in joins to do neat things, such as:

## Joining only to certain rows in the main table

The left join clause `and created_channel = 2` ensures that the join only happens 

```{.sql .interactive .subscriptions}

select 
  users.*,
  subscribers.subscription_type
from users
left join subscribers
  on user_id = users.id
  and created_channel = 2
where exists (select * from subscribers where users.id = subscribers.user_id)
limit 10

```

## Joining only certain rows from another table

```{.sql .interactive .subscriptions}

select 
  users.*,
  subscribers.subscription_type
from users
left join subscribers
  on user_id = users.id
  and subscription_type = 2
where exists (select * from subscribers where users.id = subscribers.user_id)
limit 10

```

If you're reading this book, you know about the `WHERE` clause. But there are multiple others ways to filter values!

## Anti joins

I love anti joins because they're extremely useful for a wide range of scenarios but SQL practitioners don't always learn about them early on (I certainly didn't!).

![How anti join works](../images/anti-join.gif)

## WHERE EXISTS

WHERE EXISTS isn

https://sqlperformance.com/2012/12/t-sql-queries/left-anti-semi-join

## Handling NULLs

To record missing and unknown values, SQL uses NULLs. NULL by itself is not a data value but a marker representing the absence of a value. Since NULLs are an unknown value, they will not match any value. That means, they won't work with joins, in where statements or in case when statements.

## LIKE for fixed length wildcards

When using the LIKE statement in a WHERE clause, `*` defines any number of characters. `_` defines a single character. For example, `WHERE name LIKE '_ake'` would capture *Jake* but not *Drake*.

<!-- TODO: finish exercises before publishing
## Exercises

1. Write a SQL statement that finds people with the last name ___ (come up with something where a good WHERE condition is needed with LIKE)
2. Write a SQL statement that does a semi/left-anti join
-->
